flashcards.back: E-waste refers to discarded electrical and electronic equipment (EEE) that has reached its end-of-life or is no longer wanted by the owner.
EPR is a policy approach where producers take financial and operational responsibility for collecting and recycling their products at end-of-life.

flashcards.front: What are the main categories of e-waste?
flashcards.back: The main categories include: Large household appliances (white goods), Small household appliances, IT and telecommunications equipment, Consumer electronics, Lighting equipment, Electrical tools, Toys/sports equipment, Medical devices.
Lead (in CRT monitors), Mercury (in batteries/switches), Cadmium (in rechargeable batteries), Chromium (in data tapes), Brominated flame retardants.
An international treaty designed to prevent the transfer of hazardous waste from developed to developing countries.
The process of disassembling, separating, and processing components of e-waste to recover valuable materials for reuse.
The process of recycling waste where the resulting material is of lower quality and functionality than the original.
The process of recovering valuable metals and minerals from discarded electronic devices and other waste streams.

flashcards.front: What are the 3R principles of e-waste management?
flashcards.back: Reduce (minimize waste generation), Reuse (use products again for same/different purpose), Recycle (process materials for new products).

flashcards.front: What is a Dismantling process in e-waste management?
flashcards.back: The systematic disassembly of electronic devices to separate components for material recovery and safe disposal.
The mechanical process of shredding e-waste into smaller pieces to facilitate separation and material recovery.
Chlorofluorocarbons found in refrigerators and ACs that deplete the ozone layer when released during improper e-waste disposal.

flashcards.front: What is CRT and its concern?
flashcards.back: Cathode Ray Tube - contains leaded glass and hazardous phosphors; requires special handling during disposal.
A concept promoting responsible management of electronic waste throughout the product lifecycle, involving all stakeholders.

flashcards.front: What are the health effects of improper e-waste handling?
flashcards.back: Lead causes neurological damage, Mercury affects brain/kidneys, Cadmium causes cancer, Brominated flame retardants disrupt endocrine system.
